message:
feat(btree,planner,vfs,wal,fts5,e2e): cross-crate hardening and new raptorq self-healing test

btree:
- cursor: add missing import for improved traversal support
- swiss_index: expand index logic with additional bucket handling
- swizzle: adjust pointer swizzle boundary conditions

core:
- connection: simplify and restructure connection handling (~40 fewer lines)

planner:
- decision_contract: refine contract assertion edges
- lib: adjust planner entry-point routing
- tests: extend decision contract verification coverage

vfs:
- memory: add guard for zero-length allocation edge case
- unix: clean up file-descriptor handling and error paths

pager:
- pager: tighten page-cache flush preconditions

wal:
- wal: update WAL frame-header validation logic

fts5:
- lib: add supplementary tokenizer registration hooks

harness:
- rfc6330_conformance: streamline verification assertions
- tuple_generator: reduce redundant generator scaffolding (~50 fewer lines)

e2e:
- add bd_3plop_6_raptorq_self_healing end-to-end test

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-3plop.3","title":"Fault injection: crash during compaction","description":"Fault injection test for crash during compaction (checkpoint) operations. Compaction is one of the most dangerous operations — it moves data between WAL and main database file. A crash during compaction can corrupt the database if recovery is not handled correctly.

Scenarios:
1. Crash during WAL checkpoint (wal_checkpoint): inject crash at random point during page writeback
2. Crash after partial page write (torn write): write half of a 4KB page, then crash
3. Crash during B-tree rebalance: inject crash during B-tree node split/merge
4. Crash during VACUUM: inject crash while reorganizing pages
5. Power loss simulation: kill process with SIGKILL at random intervals during compaction

Implementation:
- Use failpoints (fail crate or custom) to inject crashes at specific code locations
- Simulate torn writes by truncating file at random byte offset within a page
- After crash injection, restart database and verify recovery
- Recovery must produce a consistent database (no corruption, no missing committed data)

Verification after recovery:
- Database passes integrity check (PRAGMA integrity_check equivalent)
- All committed transactions are present
- No uncommitted transaction data is visible
- WAL is either empty (fully checkpointed) or contains valid redo records
- B-tree structure is valid

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 crash scenarios implemented with failpoints
- [ ] Recovery succeeds in all scenarios (consistent database after restart)
- [ ] No committed data lost
- [ ] No uncommitted data visible
- [ ] B-tree integrity verified after recovery
- [ ] 100 randomized crash points per scenario (statistical coverage)
- [ ] CI integration (run nightly due to time cost)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:33.552866259Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T19:13:18.292818019Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T19:13:18.292796990Z","close_reason":"Completed deterministic crash-compaction fault-injection matrix with 5 scenarios, 100-point sweep support, artifacts, and verifier script.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["durability","fault-injection","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.3","depends_on_id":"bd-ncivz","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2965,"issue_id":"bd-3plop.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Implemented deterministic crash-during-compaction fault-injection matrix (harness lane).

 {"id":"bd-3plop.4","title":"Fault injection: lock contention storms","description":"Fault injection test for extreme lock contention scenarios. With page-level MVCC and SSI, lock contention manifests differently than in traditional databases — instead of deadlocks, we see SSI abort storms. This test validates that the system remains available under extreme contention.

Scenarios:
1. Single hot row: 100 threads all update the same row concurrently (maximum SSI conflict rate)
2. Hot page: 100 threads update different rows on the same page (page-level MVCC contention)
3. Lock convoy: one slow transaction holds a page while 100 fast transactions queue behind it
4. Abort storm: deliberate write-skew patterns that cause 90%+ of transactions to abort
5. Priority inversion: high-priority read transaction blocked by low-priority writer holding epoch pin

Metrics to capture:
- Abort rate (should be bounded, not 100% for scenarios 1-3)
- Throughput under contention (operations/second, should not go to zero)
- p99 latency under contention (should be bounded, not infinite)
- Livelock detection: if forward progress stalls for > 10 seconds, flag as livelock
- Memory usage: SSI conflict tracking under high abort rates

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 scenarios complete without deadlock or livelock
- [ ] Forward progress guaranteed (at least 1 transaction commits per second)
- [ ] Abort rate bounded and documented for each scenario
- [ ] p99 latency bounded and documented
- [ ] No memory leak from aborted transaction state
- [ ] Livelock detection mechanism works","acceptance_criteria":"1. Implementation is complete and behaviorally correct against explicit invariants and edge-case requirements, including fault and recovery paths where applicable. 2. Comprehensive unit/property tests cover normal, boundary, and adversarial conditions with deterministic fixtures and regression seeds. 3. End-to-end scenario scripts validate integrated behavior in real execution flows and include deterministic replay instructions. 4. Structured logging/metrics (traceable IDs, timing, counters, error context) are emitted and validated so failures are diagnosable and auditable.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:47.022681729Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:06:12.580152692Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-pass-2026-02-13","concurrency","ev-gated","fault-injection","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2kkn4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.4","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3plop.5","title":"E2E test: SSI serialization correctness under 100 concurrent writers","description":"End-to-end test that validates SSI serialization correctness under 100 concurrent writers with a serialization checker. This is the definitive proof that page-level MVCC + SSI provides true serializable isolation.

Test design:
1. Create a database with a known initial state (e.g., bank accounts with known balances, invariant: sum of all balances = constant)
2. Launch 100 writer threads, each executing random transfer transactions (read two accounts, check constraint, write two accounts)
3. Each transaction either commits or aborts (SSI may abort for write-skew prevention)
4. After all transactions complete, verify:
   a. Sum of all balances == initial sum (invariant preserved)
   b. No negative balances if constraint was always checked
   c. Committed transaction history forms a valid serializable schedule

Serialization checker:
- Record all committed transactions with their read-sets and write-sets
- Use a cycle-detection algorithm on the serialization graph
- If a cycle exists among committed transactions: SSI BUG — serialization anomaly was allowed
- The checker is a separate validation tool, not part of the database itself

Run configuration:
- 100 writers, 10000 transactions each
- Transaction types: transfer (read 2, write 2), deposit (read 1, write 1), balance check (read all)
- Mix: 70% transfer, 20% deposit, 10% balance check
- Duration: 60 seconds minimum

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100 concurrent writers complete without deadlock
- [ ] Global invariant (sum of balances) preserved at all times
- [ ] Serialization checker finds ZERO cycles among committed transactions
- [ ] Abort rate < 20% (SSI is not overly conservative)
- [ ] Throughput > 1000 committed transactions/second
- [ ] Test runs in CI (reduced scale: 10 writers, 1000 transactions)","acceptance_criteria":"1. End-to-end scripts cover happy-path and adversarial-path behavior with deterministic seeds and one-command replay. 2. Companion unit/property tests validate harness/oracle logic to prevent false positives. 3. Structured logs must include trace_id, run_id, scenario_id, seed, timing, and first-failure diagnostics. 4. CI archives artifact bundles and fails on nondeterminism, missing logs, or oracle mismatch.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:00.562112726Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T04:30:20.373513519Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","mvcc","ssi","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2kkn4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-3plop.6","title":"E2E test: RaptorQ self-healing with 5% random page corruption","description":"End-to-end test that validates RaptorQ self-healing by corrupting 5% of database pages randomly and verifying automatic repair. This is the showcase test for FrankenSQLite's core differentiator.

Test procedure:
1. Create database with known data (1000 rows across multiple tables, known checksums)
2. Run normal workload to populate WAL and trigger checkpoint (ensures repair symbols exist)
3. Close database cleanly
4. Corrupt 5% of pages: for each target page, flip random bits (1-8 bits per page)
5. Open database
6. Read all rows (this triggers corruption detection via BLAKE3 checksum mismatch)
7. Verify: all rows read correctly (RaptorQ repair transparent to reader)
8. Verify: evidence ledger contains repair records for each corrupted page
9. Verify: BLAKE3 witness proofs in evidence ledger are valid
10. Close and reopen database: corrupted pages should be permanently repaired (not just cached)

Corruption variants:
- Random bit flips (simulating cosmic rays / bit rot)
- Zero-fill (simulating sector failure)
- Random data overwrite (simulating stray writes)
- Header corruption (page header fields)

Expected results:
- 5% corruption with 50% repair symbol overhead: 100% repair success
- 10% corruption with 50% repair symbol overhead: should still succeed (margin)
- 20% corruption: may exceed repair capacity, test graceful degradation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5% random corruption fully repaired (zero data loss)
- [ ] All corruption variants tested
- [ ] Evidence ledger entries for every repair
- [ ] BLAKE3 witness proofs valid
- [ ] Permanent repair (persisted, not just cached)
- [ ] Graceful degradation when repair capacity exceeded
- [ ] Test runs in CI (< 30 seconds)","acceptance_criteria":"1. End-to-end scripts cover happy-path and adversarial-path behavior with deterministic seeds, one-command replay, and explicit oracle checks. 2. Companion unit tests validate scenario generators, checkers, and artifact validators to prevent false positives in e2e outcomes. 3. Structured logs include trace/run/scenario identifiers, timing, resource stats, and root-cause context sufficient for post-failure triage without re-instrumentation. 4. CI archives artifact bundles (logs, manifests, checksums) and fails on nondeterminism, missing artifacts, or schema violations.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:14.565093023Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:06:01.823120140Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-pass-2026-02-13","durability","e2e","ev-gated","raptorq","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.6","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.6","depends_on_id":"bd-n0g4q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-3plop.7","title":"Conformance: run SQLite TCL test harness","description":"Set up and run the SQLite TCL test harness against FrankenSQLite for compatibility conformance. SQLite ships with ~45000 test cases in TCL that define the expected behavior for SQL execution, error handling, edge cases, and pragma behavior.

Implementation:
1. Build FrankenSQLite as a shared library with the SQLite C API surface (sqlite3_open, sqlite3_exec, etc.)
2. Point the SQLite TCL test harness at the FrankenSQLite library
3. Run the test suite, categorize results: PASS, FAIL, SKIP (unsupported feature), CRASH

Expected status:
- Core SQL tests (select, insert, update, delete): target >95% pass rate
- Transaction tests (begin, commit, rollback, savepoint): target >90% pass rate
- Error handling tests: target >90% pass rate
- Extension tests (FTS, RTREE, JSON): SKIP (not yet implemented)
- WAL tests: many will FAIL due to different WAL architecture (expected and documented)

Tracking:
- Maintain a conformance matrix: test_name, status, notes
- Track conformance percentage over time (should monotonically increase)
- Investigate and document each FAIL: is it a bug or a deliberate divergence?

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TCL test harness running against FrankenSQLite library
- [ ] Conformance matrix with pass/fail/skip counts
- [ ] Core SQL conformance >95%
- [ ] Each failure categorized as bug or deliberate divergence
- [ ] CI integration: run weekly, alert on regression (pass count decreases)
- [ ] Roadmap for closing remaining failures","acceptance_criteria":"1. End-to-end scripts cover happy-path and adversarial-path behavior with deterministic seeds and one-command replay. 2. Companion unit/property tests validate harness/oracle logic to prevent false positives. 3. Structured logs must include trace_id, run_id, scenario_id, seed, timing, and first-failure diagnostics. 4. CI archives artifact bundles and fails on nondeterminism, missing logs, or oracle mismatch.","notes":"Added explicit dependency on bd-19u.8 (SQLite C API compatibility shim) because true execute-mode TCL harness against FrankenSQLite requires sqlite3_* ABI surface and linked testfixture.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:26.814471437Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T04:30:20.057265990Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.7","depends_on_id":"bd-19u.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:48:43.867292578Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.7","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:23:24Z"},{"id":2588,"issue_id":"bd-3ppz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: TUI framework setup with frankentui and channel-based data feed

### What
Set up the TUI binary and core infrastructure using frankentui (at /dp/frankentui), with a channel-based architecture that allows background test threads to feed data to the UI.

### Architecture
```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Background Threads                               │
│  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  │
│  │Benchmark │  │Corruption│  │  Correctness   │  │
│  │ Runner   │  │ Recovery │  │  Verification  │  │
│  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └──────┬─────────┘  │
│       │              │               │            │
│       └──────────────┼───────────────┘            │
│                      │ mpsc channels               │
│                      ▼                            │
│            ┌─────────────────┐                    │
│            │  Event Loop     │                    │
│            │  (frankentui)   │                    │
│            └───────┬─────────┘                    │
│                    │                              │
│  ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  Panel 1   │  Panel 2   │  Panel 3  │ P4  │   │
│  │ Benchmark  │ Recovery   │ Correct.  │ Sum │   │
│  └────────────┴────────────┴───────────┴─────┘   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Channel Messages
```rust
pub enum DashboardEvent {
    BenchmarkProgress { name: String, ops_per_sec: f64, elapsed: Duration },
    BenchmarkComplete { name: String, results: BenchmarkResults },
    CorruptionInjected { page: u32, pattern: String },
    RecoveryAttempt { group: u32, symbols_available: u32, needed: u32 },
    RecoverySuccess { page: u32, decode_proof: String },
    RecoveryFailure { page: u32, reason: String },
    CorrectnessCheck { workload: String, frank_hash: String, csqlite_hash: String, match_: bool },
    StatusMessage(String),
}
```

### frankentui Integration
- Review frankentui's widget system and layout primitives
- Use its app framework for event loop + terminal management
- Keyboard: Tab to switch panels, q to quit, r to run/restart
- Mouse support if frankentui provides it

### Headless Mode
When `--headless` flag is passed:
- Skip TUI initialization
- Still run all tests/benchmarks
- Output JSON results to stdout or file
- For CI environments without terminal

### Acceptance Criteria
- TUI binary compiles and launches
- Shows 4 panel layout (even if panels are placeholder text)
- Channel-based data feed works (send test events, verify display updates)
- Keyboard navigation between panels
- Headless mode outputs JSON
- Terminal resize handled gracefully

profile-db — Generate JSON metadata for golden database files

USAGE:
    profile-db [OPTIONS]

OPTIONS:
    --golden-dir <DIR>    Directory containing golden .db files
                          (default: sample_sqlite_db_files/golden)
    --output-dir <DIR>    Directory for JSON output files
                          (default: sample_sqlite_db_files/metadata)
    --db <NAME>           Profile only this database file (e.g. beads_viewer.db)
    --pretty              Pretty-print JSON output
    -h, --help            Show this help message

EXAMPLES:
    profile-db
    profile-db --pretty
    profile-db --db frankensqlite.db --pretty
    profile-db --golden-dir /tmp/dbs --output-dir /tmp/meta

realdb-e2e — Differential testing of FrankenSQLite vs C SQLite

USAGE:
    realdb-e2e <SUBCOMMAND> [OPTIONS]

SUBCOMMANDS:
    corpus scan             Discover SQLite databases under /dp
    corpus import           Copy selected DBs into golden/ with checksums
    corpus verify           Verify golden copies against checksums.sha256
    run                     Execute an OpLog workload against an engine
    bench                   Run the benchmark matrix (Criterion)
    corrupt                 Inject corruption into a working copy

OPTIONS:
    -h, --help              Show this help message

EXAMPLES:
    realdb-e2e corpus scan
    realdb-e2e corpus scan --root /dp --max-depth 4
    realdb-e2e corpus import --db beads.db --tag beads
    realdb-e2e corpus verify
    realdb-e2e run --engine sqlite3 --db beads-proj-a --workload commutative_inserts --concurrency 4
    realdb-e2e run --engine fsqlite --db beads-proj-a --workload hot_page_contention --concurrency 8
    realdb-e2e bench --db beads-proj-a --preset all
    realdb-e2e corrupt --db beads-proj-a --strategy page --page 1 --seed 42

realdb-e2e corpus — Manage the SQLite database fixture corpus

USAGE:
    realdb-e2e corpus <ACTION> [OPTIONS]

ACTIONS:
    scan        Discover SQLite databases under configured roots
    import      Copy a discovered database into golden/ with checksums
    verify      Verify all golden copies match their checksums entries

SCAN OPTIONS:
    --root <DIR>        Root directory to scan (default: /dp)
    --max-depth <N>     Maximum traversal depth (default: 6)
    --header-only       Only show files with valid SQLite magic header

IMPORT OPTIONS:
    --db <NAME>         Source database path or discovery name
    --tag <LABEL>       Classification tag (beads, sample, test, etc.)

VERIFY OPTIONS:
    --checksums <PATH>  Path to checksums file (default: sample_sqlite_db_files/checksums.sha256)
